metadata.Title: Synthesis, antibacterial and antitubercular activities of benzimidazole bearing substituted 2-pyridone motifs

Accepted Manuscript
Synthesis, antibacterial and antitubercular activities of benzimidazole bearing
substituted 2-pyridone motifs
N.C. Desai , N.R. Shihory , G.M. Kotadiya , Priyanka Desai
PII: S0223-5234(14)00522-4
DOI: 10.1016/j.ejmech.2014.06.004
Reference: EJMECH 7046
To appear in: European Journal of Medicinal Chemistry
Received Date: 3 February 2014
Revised Date: 5 April 2014
Accepted Date: 3 June 2014
Please cite this article as: N. Desai, N. Shihory, G. Kotadiya, P. Desai, Synthesis, antibacterial and
antitubercular activities of benzimidazole bearing substituted 2-pyridone motifs, European Journal of
Medicinal Chemistry (2014), doi: 10.1016/j.ejmech.2014.06.004.
This is a PDF file of an unedited manuscript that has been accepted for publication. As a service to
our customers we are providing this early version of the manuscript. The manuscript will undergo
copyediting, typesetting, and review of the resulting proof before it is published in its final form. Please
note that during the production process errors may be discovered which could affect the content, and all
legal disclaimers that apply to the journal pertain.

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
• A series of benzimidazole bearing substituted 2-pyridones were synthesized.
• All the compounds were subjected for their in vitro biological screening.
• Some of the tested compounds exhibited higher potency compared to the standard.
T
• None of the tested compounds exhibited significant cytotoxic activity.
P
• Lipophilicity plays an important role in antibacterial effect of tested coImpounds.

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
1 Synthesis, antibacterial and antitubercular activities of benzimidazole bearing
2 substituted 2-pyridone motifs
3 N C Desai*, N R Shihory, G M Kotadiya, Priyanka Desai
T
4 Division of Medicinal Chemistry, Department of Chemistry
5 (UGC NON-SAP & DST-FIST Sponsored),
P
6 Mahatma Gandhi Campus, Maharaja Krishnakumarsinhji Bhavnagar University,
I
7 Bhavnagar 364 002, India
R
8 E-mail: dnisheeth@rediffmail.com,
9 Telefax: +91 278 2439852 C
10
S
U
11 Abstract
N
12 A series of benzimidazole bearing 2-pyridoAnes 5a-r were synthesized and evaluated for
13 their in vitro antibacterial and antitubercular a M ctivity. Further, all compounds were examined for
14 their cytotoxic study on VERO cell line and characterized by well-known spectral techniques. It
D
15 was observed that the compounds 5h, 5i, 5k, 5q and 5r were found to possess significant broad
E
16 spectrum antibacterial activity (12.5-100 µg/mL of MIC), while compounds 5g-5i, 5k and 5l
T
17 proved to be the most potent antitubercular activity in range of 2.76-20.4 µM of MIC at low level
P
18 of cytotoxicity, indicating good selectivity. From SAR studies, lipophilic profile of compounds
E
19 was remarkably vitaCl for antibacterial activity, while MIC values of antitubercular could not be
20 directly correlatCe with lipophilicity.
A
21 Keywords: Antibacterial activity; Antitubercular activity; Benzimidazole; Cytotoxicity; MABA
22 assay; 2-Pyridone
23
24
1

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
25 1. Introduction
26 The current first-line tuberculosis drug treatment is more than 40 years old and consists
27 primarily of rifampicin and isoniazid. These antibiotics are drug-susceptible and Trequire longer
28 time and large number of doses, which are multi-drug resistant (MDR) andP extensively drug
I
29 resistant (XDR) to tuberculosis strains [1-3]. However, the rapid increase of multi-drug-resistant
R
30 tuberculosis (MDR-TB) (resistant to at least isoniazid and rifampicin) and extensively drug-
C
31 resistant tuberculosis (XDR-TB) (resistant to isoniazid, rifampicin in addition to
S
32 fluoroquinolone, kanamycin, amikacin or capreomycin amoUng second line anti-TB drugs) has led
33 to an urgent need for the identification of new drug Ntargets and the growth of novel anti-TB
34 drugs. Furthermore, The most commonly encountAered antibiotic-resistant bacteria, methicillin-
35 resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), has M a major impact on infections in both hospitals and
36 community settings [4,5]. Unfortunately, as antibiotic resistant organisms have become more
D
37 commonplace, the pipeline for the discovery of new antimicrobial agents has decreased [6].
E
38 Thus, there is a pressing need for new antimicrobial agents that are capable of treating resistant
T
39 bacterial strains.
P
E
40 Moreover, dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) catalyzes the NADPH-dependent reduction
C
41 of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate that is essential for DNA synthesis. Inhibition of its activity
C
42 leads to arrest of DNA synthesis and hence cell death [7]. Mycobacterium tuberculosis DHFR is
A
43 an attractive novel drug target for developing anti-tuberculosis drugs. To overcome this problem,
44 we have synthesized a series of benzimidazole bearing 2-pyridones by replacing the pyrazole
45 motif in our previously synthesized compounds NCD [8] and screened them for their
1-20
46 antibacterial property. In this attempt, we got excellent antibacterial results. Antibacterial studies
2

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
47 impelled us to inspect 5a-r for their in vitro antitubercular activity as well. Structural relevance
48 of title compounds 5a-r with previously synthesized compounds is shown in Figure 1.
49 Benzimidazole nucleus is a the key building block for numerous compouTnds that play
50 beneficial roles in the functioning of biologically important molecules [9] anPd are remarkably
I
51 effective both with respect to their inhibitory activity and their favorable selectivity ratio [10-12].
R
52 Benzimidazoles are considered a promising class of bioactive heterocyclic compounds
C
53 encompassing a diverse range of biological activities such as antiulcer [13], antihelminthic [14],
S
54 antihypertensive [15], anticoagulant [16], anti-inflammatoUry [17], antimicrobial [18-20] and
55 antiparasitic [21]. The azole group of heterocyNclic compounds possesses significant
56 pharmacokinetic profile and lipophilicity that influAence the ability of drug to reach the target by
57 transmembrane diffusion and along with prom M ising activity against resistant TB by inhibiting the
58 biosynthesis of lipids [22,23]. Further, 2-pyr idones represent a unique class of pharmacophores,
D
59 which are observed in various therapeutic agents [24]. In recent years, 2-pyridones have
E
60 assimilated much importance as these compounds exhibit several biological activities such as
T
61 antitumoral [25], antimalarial [26], analgesic [27] and anti-HIV [28] properties. Moreover, 2-
P
62 pyridones are a class of recently discovered potent antibacterial agents that are of particular
E
63 interest due to their Cin vitro and in vivo antibacterial potencies against the bacterial type II DNA
64 topoisomerasesC, which include two highly homologous enzymes-DNA gyrase and topoisomerase
65 IV [29,30]A. Moreover, among the pharmacokinetic properties, a low and highly variable
66 bioavailability is indeed the main reason for stopping further development of the drug [31].
67 Motivated by the above findings and from our previous work [32,33], the main aim of the
68 work is to obtain more active antibacterial and antitubercular agents with plausible novel
69 mechanisms of action. It was thought worthwhile to synthesize some new benzimidazole bearing
3

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
70 2-pyridone derivatives comprising of the above aforementioned moieties in single molecular
71 framework in order to investigate their in vitro antibacterial and antitubercular activity. In
72 continuation to this, in our present communication, we have synthesized benzimidazole bearing
T
73 2-pyridones 5a-r and evaluated for their in vitro antibacterial and antitubercular activity. In
P
74 addition, cytotoxicity studies were also conducted in VERO cell lines to evaluate the ability of
I
R
75 these compounds to inhibit the cell growth. Most active compounds 5h, 5q and 5r were also
C
76 screened against MRSA strain.
S
77 {Insert Figure 1 here}
U
N
78 2. Results and discussion
A
79 2.1. Chemistry
M
80 We have synthesized new analogue s in which 2-pyridone motif is connected to the
D
81 benzimidazole system. The synthetic strategies adopted for the synthesis of target benzimidazole
E
82 bearing 2-pyridone derivatives 5a-r are depicted in Schemes 1 and 2. In Scheme 1, condensation
T
83 of 1-(1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-yl)ethanone 1 with equimolar quantity of cyanoacetic acid
P
84 hydrazide in refluxing 1,4-dioxane afforded a single product, that was identified as N'-(1-(1H-
E
85 benzo[d]imidazol-2C-yl)ethylidene)-2-cyanoacetohydrazide 2. The elemental analysis and spectral
86 data were in Caccordance with the proposed N'-(1-(1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-yl)ethylidene)-2-
87 cyanoacetoAhydrazide 2 structure. The IR spectrum of 2 showed strong absorption bands at 2248
88 and 1681 cm-1 were due to cyanide and carbonyl group, respectively. Its 1H NMR spectrum apart
89 from the expected aromatic signals, showed two new singlets at d 3.38 and 8.92 ppm assigned to
90 the reactive methylene protons and proton of secondary amine attached with carbonyl group
91 respectively. The 13C NMR spectrum displayed nine carbon signals, the most important signals
4

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
92 appeared at d 13.4, 27.3, 125.8, 171.3 ppm characteristic of methyl, methylene, cyanide and
93 carbonyl carbons, respectively. The mass spectrum revealed a molecular ion peak at m/z =
94 241.11 (M + 1), in agreement with its proposed structure. The presence of the reactive methylene
T
95 group in the hydrazide 2 makes it a versatile precursor for the Michael type condensation with
P
96 Knoevenagel product p-nitrobenzaldehyde and malononitrile compound I3 in presence of
R
97 catalytic amount of piperidine utilizing ethanol (95%) as a solvent furnished 2-pyridone
C
98 derivative identified as 1-((1-(1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-yl)ethylidene)amino)-6-amino-4-(4-
S
99 nitrophenyl)-2-oxo-1,2-dihydropyridine-3,5-dicarbonitrile 4. Structure of the latter product was
U
100 confirmed by IR spectra which showed characteristic absorption bands at 1688 and 3446 cm-1for
N
101 conjugated >C=O and primary amine group respectively. Their 1H NMR spectra displayed a
A
102 broad singlet at d 8.78 ppm for primary amine protons, besides the disappearance of reactive
M
103 methylene group and secondary amine singlets due to its involvement in cyclization.
D
104 Condensation of 2-pyridone derivative 4 with appropriate aromatic aldehydes in boiling ethanol
E
105 afforded the respective targeted benzimidazole bearing 2-pyridones acknowledged as 1-((1-(1H-
T
106 benzo[d]imidazol-2-yl)ethylidene)amino)-6-((arylbenzylidene)amino)-4-(4-nitrophenyl)-2-oxo-
P
107 1,2-dihydropyridine-3,5-dicarbonitriles 5a-r. The structures of the final analogues 5a-r were
E
108 established by IR spectra which showed disappearance of -NH band in compound 4. A strong
2
C
109 absorption band appeared between 1679-1688 cm-1 was assigned to conjugated >C=O group.
C
110 Their 1H NMR spectra revealed a singlet of methine proton at d 9.40-9.57 ppm involved in
A
111 azomethine formation, along with the vanishing of primary amine singlet. The 13C NMR
112 spectrum of compound 5l, displayed, besides the expected methyl and aromatic signals, three
113 characteristic signals at d 115.9, 160.1 and 163.8 ppm due to the carbons of CN, C=O and CH=N
114 respectively. The mass spectrum of 5l showed molecular ion peak at m/z = 540.17 (M + 1), in
5

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
115 agreement with its proposed structure. Similarly, the spectral values for all the compounds and
116 C, H, N analysis are presented in the experimental part.
117 {Insert Scheme 1 here} T
P
118 A plausible mechanistic pathway for the formation of compounds 5a-r is suggested in
I
R
119 Scheme 2. Firstly, hydrazone (A) underwent Michael addition with Knoevenagel product (B)
C
120 and furnished the intermediate (C), which further experienced intramolecular nucleophilic attack
121 on cyanide carbon followed by annulation to yield intermed S iate (D). The intermediate (D)
U
122 transformed to compound (E) by intramolecular electron transfer to nitrogen atom. In the last
N
123 step, intermediate (E) was transformed to targeted compounds by intermolecular nucleophilic
A
124 attack on carbonyl carbon of different aromatic aldehydes.
M
125 {Insert Scheme 2 here}
D
126 2.2. Biological Evaluation
E
T
127 2.2.1. Antibacterial activity
P
128 The intermediateEs 2, 4 and target compounds 5a-r were investigated for their in vitro
129 antibacterial activityC against two gram-positive and two gram-negative bacteria and the yeast like
130 pathogenic funCgus Candida albicans using conventional broth-dilution method [34,35].
131 Minimum A inhibitory concentration (MIC) is defined as the concentration of the compound
132 required to obtain complete inhibition of bacterial growth. MICs of the synthesized compounds
133 were compared with ciprofloxacin and chloramphenicol and the results were depicted in Table 1.
134 The antibacterial activity assessed (Table 1) for analogues 2, 4 and 5a-r against several strains
135 flaunted good activity. From the bioassay, it was observed that the final analogues 5a-r with the
6

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
136 substitutions of phenyl ring were most active against all the pathogenic strains studied than the
137 intermediate hydrazone 2 and 2-pyridone derivative 4. Intermediate 2 showed very poor
138 antibacterial activity against all tested bacterial strains and it was observed that compound 2 with
T
139 low lipophilicity (Clog P = 0.7220) displayed very low activity as compared to intermediate 4
P
140 and final analogues 5a-r. Precursor 2 reacted with Knoevenagel compound 3 to generate
I
R
141 intermediate 4 which was tested against different bacterial strains, and it showed more inhibitory
C
142 efficacy than compound 2 due to high lipophilicity (Clog P = 1.4772) and less activity compared
S
143 to all final analogues. Therefore, it was observed that formation of 2-pyridone nucleus was
U
144 responsible for enhancing antibacterial potency. Now, intermediate 4 was treated with different
N
145 aromatic aldehydes to afford targeted compounds 5a-r which were found to have broad spectrum
A
146 antimicrobial efficacy. This could be correlated to structural variations and different aromatic
M
147 substitutions in phenyl ring.
D
148 Good bioavailability can be achieved by an appropriate balance between solubility and
E
149 partitioning properties. Membrane permeability and bioavailability is always associated with
T
150 some basic molecular descriptor such as Clog P (lipophilicity). Thus, prediction of
P
151 bioavailability-related properties, such as lipophilicity is important before actual synthesis, in
E
152 order to reduce enoCrmous wastage of expensive chemicals and precious time. The computed
153 Clog P values C(P is the partition coefficient of the molecule in the water/octanol system) are
154 shown in TAable 1 and 3. The ChemBioDraw Ultra, version 12.0, software by Cambridge Soft is
155 a program used to predict lipophilicity of compounds.
156 From the bioassay, final analogues with electron withdrawing halogen substitution were
157 found to be more active than the remaining final analogues against all the bacterial strains.
158 Among all the final active analogues, compound 5q (3-Br, Clog P = 3.9272) endowed with
7

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
159 bromine exerted highest inhibition against all the bacterial strains and showed highest efficacy
160 against S. aureus at 12.5 µg/mL of MIC as compared to standards ciprofloxacin (50 µg/mL, Clog
161 P = -0.7252) and chloramphenicol (50 µg/mL, Clog P = 1.293). In addition, compound 5q (Clog
T
162 P = 3.9272) with higher lipophilicity flaunted potential inhibitory action against E. coli and S.
P
163 pyogenes at MIC of 25 µg/mL. Moreover, the above mentioned derivative 5q displayed excellent
I
R
164 inhibition against P. aeruginosa at 25 µg/mL of MIC. Furthermore, among the second line active
C
165 compounds, 5h (3-Cl, Clog P = 3.7772) and 5r (4-Br, Clog P = 3.9272) indicated MIC of 50
S
166 µg/mL, in which more lipophilic compound 5r showed better results against both E. coli and S.
U
167 pyogenes. In addition, the above mentioned derivatives 5h and 5r were also found to
N
168 demonstrate excellent efficacy against S. aureus at 25 µg/mL of MIC and analogue 5r also
A
169 demonstrated MIC of 50 µg/mL against P. aeruginosa. Derivatives 5e (3-F, Clog P = 3.2072)
M
170 with fluoro, 5g (2-Cl, Clog P = 3.7772) with chloro and 5k (3-CH , Clog P = 3.5632) with
3
171 methyl group to the aromatic nucleus D bequeathed appreciable potency towards E. coli at 100
172 µg/mL of MIC, in which compouEnd 5g showing highest lipophilicity gave better results.
T
173 Compound 5h (3-Cl, Clog P = 3.7772) with electron withdrawing chlorine substituent indicated
P
174 diminished activity at 62.5 µg/mL of MIC against P. aeruginosa. Another analogue 5i (4-Cl,
E
175 Clog P = 3.7772) with chlorine and 5k (3-CH , Clog P = 3.5632) endowed with electron
3
C
176 donating methyl group exerted MIC of 100 µg/mL against P. aeruginosa as compared to
C
177 standard ciprofloxacin (25 µg/mL, Clog P = -0.7252) and chloramphenicol (50 µg/mL, Clog P =
A
178 1.293) antibiotics. Derivative 5g (2-Cl, Clog P = 3.7772) displayed more potency against S.
179 aureus than E. coli at MIC of 50 µg/mL and diminished activity at MIC of 62.5 µg/mL against S.
180 pyogenes. Final derivatives 5c (3-OH, Clog P = 3.2982), 5e (3-F, Clog P = 3.2072), 5k (3-CH ,
3
181 Clog P = 3.5632) and 5m (3-OCH , Clog P = 3.4082) displayed remarkable inhibitory effects at
3
8

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
182 MIC of 100 µg/mL against S. aureus. Among them compound 5k contemplated same action
183 against S. pyogenes. In addition, compound 5i (4-Cl, Clog P = 3.7772) exhibited activity at MIC
184 of 50 and 62.5 µg/mL against S. pyogenes and S. aureus respectively. Moreover, compound 5e
T
185 possessing fluorine atom at 3rd position of phenyl ring increased the potency and showed
P
186 inhibition at 62.5 µg/mL of MIC against S. pyogenes. Furthermore, the inhibitory activity of all
I
R
187 the synthesized compounds against C. albicans was rather lower than their antibacterial activity,
C
188 only compound 5q (3-Br, MIC = 250 µg/mL) exhibited moderate activity as compared to
S
189 standard ketoconazole.
U
190 {Insert Table 1 hNere}
A
191 Furthermore, the most active compounds 5h, 5q and 5r against S. aureus and S. pyogenes
M
192 (MIC = 12.5-50 µg/mL) were also tested against methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA isolate
193 ATCC 43300) and results are given inD Table 2. Compound 5r exhibited more potent activity
194 than the standard drugs against methEicillin-resistant S. aureus. Compound 5r, with MIC value of
195 6.25 µg/mL against MRSA shTowed four-fold more potency than ciprofloxacin (MIC = 25
196 µg/mL) and eight-fold morPe activity than chloramphenicol (MIC = 50 µg/mL). In addition,
E
197 compound 5q endowed with bromo group showed two-fold more inhibition at MIC value of 12.5
C
198 µg/mL than ciprofloxacin and four-fold higher potency than chloramphenicol against MRSA.
C
199 A {Insert Table 2 here}
200 2.2.2. Antitubercular activity
201 The encouraging results from the antibacterial studies impelled us to go for the screening
202 of title compounds for their in vitro antitubercular activity. Intermediates (2 and 4) and all the
9

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
203 final compounds along with the standard drug for comparison were firstly evaluated for their
204 activity against the Mycobacterium tuberculosis H Rv strain in Middlebrook 7H12 medium
37
205 using Microplate Alamar Blue Assay (MABA) MIC method [36]. The drug in clinical use,
T
206 isoniazid was used as a reference drug. The results of actual MICs of tested compounds were
P
207 reported in Table 3. It was observed that final analogues 5a-r displayed superior anti-tubercular
I
R
208 activity compared to both the intermediates (2 and 4). Among the eighteen synthesized
C
209 compounds, compounds 5g-5i, 5k and 5l endowed with inductively electron withdrawing
S
210 chlorine and electron donating methyl groups afforded maximum MICs ranging from 0.85 to
U
211 6.28 µg/mL against Mycobacterium tuberculosis. For final derivatives, introduction of chlorine
N
212 substituent in 5g-5i was tolerated, 3-chloro yielding the best activity (3-Cl > 4-Cl > 2-Cl).
A
213 Furthermore, among the second line active compounds, 5k and 5l brandished MICs in range of
M
214 2.07-6.28 µg/mL, in which compound 5k having methyl in meta position showed better result at
215 MIC of 2.07 µg/mL whereas compo D und 5l displayed inhibition at MIC of 6.28 µg/mL.
216 Compound 5h (3-Cl) possessed higEhest inhibition at MIC of 0.85 µg/mL amongst all the tested
T
217 derivatives as compared to standard isoniazid, while their ortho and para derivatives 5i (4-Cl)
P
218 and 5g (2-Cl) showed MICs of 1.79 and 2.65 µg/mL respectively. Here, we have discussed and
E
219 compared antitubercular activity based on first line drug Isoniazid (0.24 µg/mL, Clog P = -
C
220 0.668). From the antitubercular activity results, it may be concluded that final derivatives 5a-o
C
221 were mixdly active and no specific relationship was observed in case of Clog P or lipophilicity
A
222 verses antitubercular activity profiles.
223 2.2.3. Cytotoxicity study
224 After having identified numerous active antibacterial and antimycobacterial
225 benzimidazole bearing 2-pyridones, the next step was to examine the toxicity of the drug
10

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
226 contenders. In vitro cytotoxicity of compounds 5a-r was determined against mammalian VERO
227 cell lines [37]. After 72 h of exposure, viability was considered on the basis of cellular
228 conversion of [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-
T
229 tetrazolium and phenylmethasulfazone] (100:20) into formazan product using Promega Cell Titer
P
230 96 non-radioactive cell proliferation assay. The cytotoxicity results presented in Table 3 are
I
R
231 expressed as the concentration inhibiting 50% of the cell growth IC . The compounds exhibited
50
C
232 moderate to low level of cytotoxicity with the IC values in the range of 13.73 - >39.42 µg/mL.
50
S
233 It was observed that none of the tested compounds exhibited any significant cytotoxic effects on
U
234 VERO cells, suggesting a great potential for their in vivo use as antibacterial and anti-tubercular
N
235 agents. As for activity against VERO cell lines, the highest cytotoxic activity was flaunted by
A
236 compound 5d which showed percentage viability IC at 13.73 µg/mL, whereas, the second line
50
M
237 cytotoxic activity was displayed by compounds 5a, 5c and 5p which showed the percentage
238 viability IC at 17.61, 17.00 and 16.05 µ D g/mL respectively. Moreover, final analogues 5g-5i, 5k,
50
239 5l, 5q and 5r showed no toxicity forE the percentage viability IC > 39.42 µg/mL.
50
T
240 {Insert Table 3 here}
P
E
241 2.3. Structure-activity relationship
C
242 The results of antibacterial and antitubercular screening demonstrated the following
C
243 assumptionAs about the structural activity relationship (SAR): in the present study, we
244 investigated the effects of the substitution pattern of the hybrid benzimidazole and 2-pyridone
245 derivatives were carefully selected to impart different electronic environment on the molecules.
246 By comparing the antibacterial activity of the synthesized compounds, it was found that the
247 tested compounds were more effective against Gram-positive bacteria. In addition, the
11

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
248 antibacterial activity was considerably affected by substitution pattern on the phenyl ring and
249 lipophilic profile of the compounds. It is believed that the strong lipophilic character of the
250 molecule plays an essential role in producing antibacterial effect. These properties are seen as an
T
251 important parameter related to membrane permeation in biological system. Many processes of
P
252 drug disposition depend on the capability to cross membranes and hence there is a high
I
R
253 correlation with measures of lipophilicity. Lipophilicity plays a major role in determining where
C
254 drugs are distributed within the body after adsorption and as a consequence how rapidly they are
S
255 metabolized and excreted. In this context, the presence of lipophilic moiety would be important
U
256 for such activity. In case of antibacterial activity, some analogues of this series were found to
N
257 have even more potency than the standard drug ‘chloramphenicol’ while some of them exhibited
A
258 comparable potency. Compounds 5h, 5i, 5k, 5q and 5r bearing Cl, Br, CH groups were found to
3
M
259 be most potent antibacterial agents due to its high lipophilicity. Among them, compound 5q
260 endowed with inductively electron with D drawing bromine at meta position emerged as the most
261 effective antibacterial agent at MICE of 12.5 µg/mL. The MIC values of these novel compounds
T
262 confirmed that the presence of bromine, chlorine and methyl substituent at meta position gave
P
263 rise to better pharmacological potency rather than the same substituent present at ortho or para
E
264 positions. Whereas, the opposite trend was observed for antitubercular activity results and there
C
265 was no specific relationship observed between MIC profiles versus lipophilicity. Among the
C
266 eighteen synthesized compounds, 5g-5i, 5k and 5l endowed with inductively electron
A
267 withdrawing chlorine and electron donating methyl group illustrated maximum MICs in the
268 range of 0.85 to 6.28 µg/mL against Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Out of them, 3-chloro yielded
269 the best activity at MIC of 0.85 µg/mL as compared to first line drug isoniazid (0.24 µg/mL).
270 3. Conclusion
12

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
271 The focal point of the current work was on the development of new bioactive based on
272 benzimidazole clubbed 2-pyridone compact system with the hope of generating new bioactive
273 chemical entities that could be useful as potent antibacterial and antitubercular agents. Many of
T
274 the synthesized motifs (5h, 5i, 5k, 5q and 5r), possessing atom/group such as bromo, chloro and
P
275 methyl at meta or para positions were identified as the most compelling antibacterial agents.
I
R
276 Albeit, it was observed that the strong lipophilic character of the molecule plays an imperative
C
277 role in producing antibacterial effect. Compounds 5g-5i, 5k and 5l came out as the most
S
278 promising antitubercular agents. In addition, the relationship between activity profiles and
U
279 lipophilicity of the newer analogues was also discussed in which higher lipophilic compounds
N
280 showed higher bioactivities, while, in case of antitubercular activity, there was no specific
A
281 relationship observed between MIC profiles versus lipophilicity. Moreover, the potent
M
282 antibacterial and antitubercular activity of most active compounds 5g-5i, 5k, 5l, 5q and 5r were
283 accompanied with relatively low level o D f cytotoxicity. Consequently, the degree of activity and
284 the encouraging physicochemical Eparameters displayed by a novel innovative structural
T
285 combination system of benzimidazole and 2-oxopyridine rings make such compounds a
P
286 privileged structure to achieve more active derivatives in ongoing studies. Synthesized
E
287 compounds were screened against C. albicans, but it was our observation that none of the
C
288 compounds exhibited significant activity against this fungi.
C
289 4. ExperimAental
290 4.1. General Methods
291 All reactions except those in aqueous media were carried out by standard techniques for
292 the exclusion of moisture. Melting points were determined on an electro thermal melting point
13

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
293 apparatus and were reported uncorrected. TLC on silica gel plates (Merck, 60, F ) was used for
254
294 purity checking and reaction monitoring. Column chromatography on silica gel (Merck, 70-230
295 mesh and 230-400 mesh ASTH for flash chromatography) was applied when necessary to isolate
T
296 and purify the reaction products. Elemental analysis (% C, H, N) was carried out by a Perkin-
P
297 Elmer 2400 CHN analyzer. IR spectra of all compounds were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR
I
R
298 spectrophotometer in KBr. 1H NMR and spectra were recorded on Varian Gemini 400 MHz and
C
299 13C NMR spectra on Varian Mercury-400, 100 MHz in DMSO-d as a solvent and
6
S
300 tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal standard. Mass spectra were scanned on a Shimadzu LC-
U
301 MS 2010 spectrometer. Anhydrous reactions were carried out in oven-dried glassware in
N
302 nitrogen atmosphere.
A
303 4.2. General procedure for the synthesis M of N'-(1-(1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-yl)ethylidene)-2-
304 cyanoacetohydrazide (2)

551 4.5. BiologiAcal assay
552 4.5.1. Antibacterial assay
553 Antibacterial studies of newly synthesized compounds 5a-r were carried out against the
554 representative panel of bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus MTCC-96, Streptococcus
25

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
555 pyogenes MTCC-442, Escherichia coli MTCC-443, Pseudomonas aeruginosa MTCC-1688 and
556 methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA isolate ATCC 43300). Antifungal activity was carried out
557 against the yeast-like pathogenic fungus Candida albicans MTCC 227. All MTCC and ATCC
T
558 cultures were collected from Institute of Microbial Technology, Chandigarh. The activity of
P
559 compounds was determined as per National Committee for Clinical Laboratory Standards
I
R
560 (NCCLS) protocol using Mueller Hinton Broth (Becton Dickinson, USA). Primary screening
C
561 was done first for antibacterial activity in six sets against E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and S.
S
562 pyogenes at different concentrations of 1000, 500, 250 µg/mL. The compounds found to be
U
563 active in primary screening were similarly diluted to obtain 200, 125, 100, 62.5, 50, 25, 12.5 and
N
564 6.25 µg/mL concentrations for secondary screening to test in a second set of dilution against all
A
565 microorganisms. Inoculum size for test strain was adjusted to 106 CFU/mL (Colony Forming
M
566 Unit per milliliter) by comparing the turbidity (turbidimetric method). Mueller Hinton Broth was
567 used as nutrient medium to grow and d D ilute the compound suspension for test organisms. 2%
568 DMSO was used as a diluent/veEhicle to obtain the desired concentration of synthesized
T
569 compounds and standard drugs to test upon standard microbial strains. Synthesized compounds
P
570 were diluted to 1000 µg/mL concentration, as stock solution. The control tube containing no
E
571 antibiotic was immediately subcultured [before inoculation] by spreading a loopful evenly over a
C
572 quarter of plate in a suitable medium for the growth of test organisms. The culture tubes were
C
573 then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and the growth was monitored visually and
A
574 spectrophotometrically. Ten µg/mL suspensions were further inoculated on an appropriate media
575 and growth was noted after 24 h and 48 h. The lowest concentration (highest dilution) required to
576 arrest the growth of bacteria was regarded as minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) i.e. the
577 amount of growth from the control tube before incubation (which represents the original
26

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
578 inoculum) was compared. Solvent had no influence on strain growth. The result of this was
579 greatly affected by the size of the inoculums. The test mixture should contain 106 CFU/mL
580 organisms. DMSO and sterilized distilled water were used as negative control while
T
581 chloramphenicol (1 U strength) was used as positive control. Standard drugs used in the present
P
582 study were ‘ciprofloxacin’ and ‘chloramphenicol’ for evaluating antibacterial activity.
I
R
583 4.5.2. Antitubercular assay
C
584 Antitubercular activity was determined using the mo S dified radiometric 7H12 broth
U
585 (BACTEC 12B system) in which stock solutions as test compounds were prepared in
N
586 dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) at a concentration of 12.8 mM and the final test concentrations
A
587 ranged from 39.42 to 0.15 µg/mL. Controls received 50 µL DMSO. INH was included as a
M
588 positive drug control. INH was solubilized and diluted in DMSO and added to BACTEC-12
589 broth to achieve a range of concentrationD for determination of minimum inhibitory concentration
590 (MIC, lowest concentration inhibitinEg ≥ 90% of the inoculums, MIC value of INH is 0.24 µg/mL
591 at 95% inhibition of H Rv straiTn). M. tuberculosis H Rv strain (ATCC 27294) was cultured at
37 37
592 37 ˚C in 100 ml of MiddlePbrook 7H9 broth (Difco, Detroit, Mich.) supplemented with 0.2%
E
593 (vol/vol) glycerol, 10% (vol/vol) OADC (oleic acid, albumin, dextrose, catalase) and 0.05%
C
594 (vol/vol) Tween 80. The complete medium was referred to as 7H9GC-Tween. Cultures were
C
595 incubated in 500-ml nephelometer flasks on a rotary shaker at 150 rpm and 37 °C until they
A
596 reached an optical density of 0.4 to 0.5 at 550 nm. Bacteria were washed and suspended in 20 ml
597 of phosphate-buffered saline and passed through an 8-mm pore size filter to eliminate clumps.
598 The filtrates were aliquoted, stored at 280 °C and used within 30 days. Cultures were prepared
599 and an appropriate dilution performed such that a BACTEC-12B vial inoculated with 0.1 mL
600 would reach a growth index (GI) of 999 in five days. Antimicrobial susceptibility testing was
27

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT
601 performed in black, clear-bottomed, 96-well microplates in order to minimize background
602 fluorescence. Outer perimeter wells were filled with sterile water to prevent dehydration in
603 experimental wells. Initial drug dilutions were prepared in either dimethyl sulfoxide or distilled
T
604 deionized water and subsequent two fold dilutions were performed in 0.1 ml of 7H9GC (no
P
605 Tween 80) in the microplates. BACTEC 12B-passaged inocula were initially diluted 1:2 in
I
R
606 7H9GC, and 0.1 ml was added to wells. Subsequent determination of bacterial titers yielded 1 ×
C
607 106 CFU/mL in plate wells for H Rv. Frozen inocula were initially diluted 1:20 in BACTEC
37
S
608 12B medium followed by a 1:50 dilution in 7H9GC. Addition of 1/10 mL to wells resulted in
U
609 final bacterial titers of 2.0 × 105 for H Rv. Wells containing drug only were used to detect auto
37
N
610 fluorescence of compounds. Cultures were incubated at 37 °C and the Growth of Inhibition (GI)
A
611 determined daily until control cultures achieved a GI of 999. Assays were usually completed in
M
612 5-8 days. Percent inhibition was defined as 1-(GI of test sample/GI of control) 100. The lowest
613 drug concentration effecting an inhibitio D n of ≥ 90% was considered the MIC.

2
C

A
619 sulfoxide (DMSO) and further dilutions were made with fresh culture medium. The

621 viability. In a transparent 96-well plate (Falcon Micro test 96), three fold serial dilutions of the
